figures.caption: Fig. 3. Overview of our pipeline. RaTA-Tool supports multimodal user queries by jointly processing the input prompt and associated modalities. A fine-tuned LLM encodes the combined inputs to generate a structured task description of the user request, further aligned via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). This description is embedded and used to retrieve the most relevant tool from an external tool collection.

Table 3. Ablation study on the effect of tool-description format and prompting or finetuning strategy on tool-selection performance, reported for both retrieval backbones (Contriever and Qwen3-Embedding). Results are shown for text, image, and audio queries, together with unweighted (Avgq) and weighted (Avgm) aggregate metrics.
Table 2. Accuracy results on aggregate metrics: unweighted average across modalities (Avgq) and weighted average (Avgm).
Fig. 8. Example of a JSON-style prompt used for model description generation during dataset creation.

Fig. 1. Pipeline of dataset creation. We first perform a metadata acquisition step by scraping model cards from Hugging Face. The collected data then undergoes a cleaning stage. Finally, given a user query and its associated prompt, we feed this information into an LLM to generate a structured tool description in a standardized JSON format.
Fig. 9. Example of a JSON-style prompt used by RaTA-Tool for task-description generation at inference time.
Fig. 6. Additional qualitative examples of RaTA-Tool for multimodal user queries with image inputs.
Fig. 7. Qualitative comparison of task descriptions with zero-shot inference and by RaTA-Tool, along with the ground-truth descriptions.
Fig. 4. Qualitative results of RaTA-Tool under different input modalities.
Fig. 10. Examples of NL-style prompts used for model description generation during dataset creation and for task-description generation with RaTA-Tool at inference time.
Fig. 2. Qualitative dataset examples illustrating Hugging Face model cards and the corresponding tool descriptions represented in the standardized JSON format.
Fig. 5. Additional qualitative examples of RaTA-Tool for text-only user queries.
